title: Paczki
subtitle: Polish deep-fried enriched doughnuts filled with plum jam
excerpt: A Polish carnival doughnut eaten on Fat Thursday (Tłusty Czwartek) before Lent: larger and richer than a standard berliner, with extra egg yolks and a small amount of spirit in the dough to give a particularly tender, brioche-like interior. Filled with a thick plum or rosehip jam, deep-fried, glazed with icing sugar, and sometimes finished with a strip of candied orange peel.
sourceNotes: Paczki are documented in Polish culinary literature from at least the seventeenth century as a carnival sweet eaten before the Lenten fast. Jan Chryzostom Pasek, Pamietniki (seventeenth-century Polish memoirs, now in the public domain), references carnival confections including paczki. Maria Ochorowicz-Monatowa, Polska kuchnia (1913, Polish culinary standard now in the public domain under Polish copyright law), gives a detailed paczki recipe with egg yolks, spirits, and plum jam filling.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Paczki must be eaten on the day of frying. The proved doughnuts can be refrigerated overnight; bring to room temperature for 45 minutes before frying.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: Deep-fried in vegetable oil at 175°C. Paczki are larger than berliners; allow 3 to 4 minutes per side.

recipeTools.notes: With dough hook; strongly recommended for this very enriched dough.

glossaryTerms.definition: Paczki dough is considerably richer than a standard doughnut dough: it uses only egg yolks (no whole eggs) for a deeper yellow colour and a more tender, fat-enriched crumb; a small amount of rum or spirit, which evaporates during frying and leaves a slight flavour; and a higher butter-to-flour ratio than a berliner. The dough is correspondingly slower to rise and more difficult to knead by hand. It should be kneaded until it pulls away cleanly from the bowl, and should be springy and satiny, not sticky. Proofed paczki float visibly higher than berliners in the oil.

content.text: Paczki are distinguished from berliners primarily by

: egg yolks only, more butter, and a small amount of rum. The resulting doughnut is noticeably lighter inside and deeper in colour than a berliner. A stand mixer is strongly recommended; the high fat content makes hand-kneading to full development tiring.

items.prepNote: plum or rosehip jam, thick and smooth
mixed with 2 tablespoons warm water for glaze

content.text: Combine flour, yeast, salt, and sugar. Add warm milk, egg yolks, vanilla, and rum and mix to a rough dough. Knead for 10 minutes until smooth. Work in the soft butter a piece at a time; knead for a further 6 to 8 minutes until the dough is smooth, satiny, and pulls away cleanly from the bowl. Cover and leave for 60 minutes until doubled.

Divide into 12 pieces (about 90 g each). Shape each into a smooth ball, pressing a small indent in the centre and placing a teaspoon of jam inside, then sealing and re-rolling into a smooth ball. Alternatively, fill after frying. Arrange on lined trays, cover with oiled film, and prove for 50 minutes until puffed.

Heat the oil to 175°C. Fry 3 at a time for 3 to 4 minutes per side until deep mahogany brown. Drain briefly on paper. Mix the icing sugar with enough warm water for a pourable glaze. Dip the top of each warm paczek in the glaze or drizzle over. If filling after frying, push a piping nozzle into the side and pipe in the jam while still warm.
